title: The Call Felt Fine. The Buyer Mentally Left 20 Minutes Earlier.

seo.metaTitle: Why Good Calls Don't Close | Business Impact Canada
seo.metaDescription: Forensic analysis of recorded sales calls: where buyers quietly disengage, drift markers, and the exact moment deals die before the call ends.

components.eyebrow: Forensic analysis of recorded sales calls
components.headline: The call felt fine. The buyer mentally left 20 minutes earlier.
components.headlineHighlight: The buyer mentally left 20 minutes earlier.
components.subheadline: Most lost deals don't end with rejection. They end with fake agreement — and the recording shows exactly when.
bullets: See where buyers quietly disengage — before they say a word
Catch the exact moment trust drops (it's never when you think)
Why your best calls are still ending in ghosting
Spot the invisible pipeline leaks draining your revenue right now
components.microcopy: 11-page forensic evidence file · Used by closers, founders, and sales teams losing deals after calls that felt fine.

exchanges.speaker: BUYER
exchanges.line: Yeah — we're trying to get something in place before Q3. Board meeting, real urgency.

exchanges.speaker: BUYER
exchanges.line: Yeah… it's a little higher than we'd budgeted for.
exchanges.annotation: Response dropped from 28 words → 12. Hesitation trail begins here.

exchanges.speaker: BUYER
exchanges.line: Send it over. We're pretty interested.
exchanges.annotation: Exit phrase delivered as progress. Ghost composing.

forensicArtifact.verdict: Deal gone at 19:08. Salesperson kept selling for 22 more minutes.

components.answer: A good sales call can feel productive while the buyer has already mentally exited. Forensic call analysis identifies drift, hesitation trails, and fake agreement from the recording — not from CRM stage or rep intuition.

components.heading: This isn't a coaching problem. It's a revenue leak.
components.body: If two deals per month die from invisible conversational drift — not bad fit, not price objections, but drift you never detected — here's the number.

stat.premise: One invisible conversational leak. Two deals a month.

components.fileLabel: Case File · High-Ticket Discovery Call · B2B SaaS
paragraphs: The founder thought the call went well.
The buyer nodded. Asked questions. Sounded invested. He logged off and updated his CRM to 80% probability.
Sherpa flagged the exact moment authority collapsed: minute 14:33 — when a mild pricing question triggered overexplaining.
The buyer mentally exited the deal 22 minutes before the call ended.

verdictRows.label: Pattern
verdictRows.value: Authority Collapse → Hesitation Trail → Fake Agreement

components.headline: The recording already has the answer.
components.subtext: Download the 11-page forensic evidence file before your next follow-up goes to someone who already decided.

components.text: Business Impact Canada is a nonprofit research organization analyzing conversational drift in recorded B2B sales calls.

components.heading: Where the deal actually changed
components.subheading: Most reps never see these on replay. The transcript marks all four.

moments.title: Genuine urgency — still evaluating
moments.description: Buyer offers timeline and board pressure. Full sentences. Forward energy.
moments.snippet: We're trying to get something in place before Q3.

moments.title: Tone shift — hesitation trail opens
moments.description: Response contracts. Elongated yeah. First pause before answering.
moments.snippet: Yeah… it's a little higher than we'd budgeted for.

moments.title: Authority collapse
moments.description: Six words. Buyer shifted from evaluating to enduring.

moments.title: Fake agreement
moments.description: Exit phrase delivered as progress. Ghost already composing.
moments.snippet: Send it over. We're pretty interested.

exchanges.salesperson: Does the pricing feel in range?
exchanges.buyer: Yeah… it's a little higher than we'd budgeted for.

marker.label: TONE SHIFTS HERE
marker.note: The salesperson heard a budget objection. The recording shows a psychological exit.

components.attribution: Composite example — patterns from recorded B2B conversations. Anonymized.

components.heading: The call felt fine because nothing sounded wrong

blocks.title: CRM updates on tone, not trajectory
blocks.body: Nods and questions read as progress. The buyer's word count and pause length told a different story.

blocks.title: Objections get coached, not forensically read
blocks.body: Budget pushback at 19:08 was the start of drift — not a pricing problem to solve with more talking.

components.closing: The moment is in the transcript. Most salespeople never find it without a forensic read.

faqs.question: What is forensic sales call analysis?
faqs.answer: Structured review of recorded calls for drift, hesitation trails, and exit phrases — not generic scoring or talk ratios alone.

faqs.question: Do I need Gong or Fathom?
faqs.answer: A transcript or recording link is enough. We accept exports from common recording tools.

components.heading: Download the 11-page forensic breakdown
paragraphs: Walkthrough of drift, collapse, and fake agreement — with annotated transcript excerpts.
